questions.q: What does the 'C. V.' in C. V. Raman's name stand for?
questions.explanation: His full name was Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman.

questions.q: In which scientific field did C. V. Raman make his most famous discovery?
questions.explanation: Raman was a physicist whose work on the scattering of light earned him the Nobel Prize in Physics.

questions.q: The phenomenon Raman discovered is famously known as the:
questions.explanation: His discovery of inelastic scattering of light is called the Raman Effect.

questions.q: In which year did C. V. Raman win the Nobel Prize?
questions.explanation: Raman was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1930.

questions.q: C. V. Raman was the first person from which continent to win a Nobel Prize in the sciences?
questions.explanation: Raman was the first Asian to win a Nobel Prize in any of the sciences.

questions.q: The Raman Effect describes the scattering of what?
questions.explanation: The Raman Effect concerns the inelastic scattering of light by molecules.

questions.q: What is the name of the day celebrated in India to commemorate Raman's discovery?
questions.explanation: National Science Day is celebrated in India on 28 February, the date Raman announced his discovery in 1928.

questions.q: In which country was C. V. Raman born?
questions.explanation: Raman was born in Tiruchirappalli (then Trichinopoly), in southern India.

questions.q: Observations of the blue colour of which natural feature partly inspired Raman's research on light scattering?
questions.explanation: Raman became fascinated by the blue colour of the Mediterranean Sea on a 1921 voyage, sparking his studies of light scattering.

questions.q: What civilian honour, India's highest, was conferred on C. V. Raman in 1954?
questions.explanation: Raman was among the first recipients of the Bharat Ratna, India's highest civilian award, in 1954.

questions.q: On what date in 1928 did Raman announce his discovery of the effect, now commemorated as National Science Day?
questions.explanation: Raman announced the discovery on 28 February 1928, which India observes as National Science Day.

questions.q: Which younger Indian physicist worked closely with Raman and co-observed the effect in 1928?
questions.explanation: K. S. Krishnan was Raman's collaborator in the 1928 experiments that revealed the Raman Effect.

questions.q: At which Kolkata institution did Raman conduct much of his early research before moving to Bangalore?
options: Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science
questions.explanation: Raman did pioneering work at the Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science (IACS) in Calcutta.

questions.q: Before becoming a full-time physicist, Raman worked for a decade in which profession?
questions.explanation: Raman served in the Indian Finance Department (Accounts Service) for about ten years while pursuing physics in his spare time.

questions.q: When the Raman Effect occurs, the scattered light differs from the incident light in what property?
options: Only its polarization angle by exactly 90 degrees
questions.explanation: In Raman scattering a small fraction of light is shifted in frequency (wavelength) due to energy exchange with molecular vibrations.

questions.q: Which prestigious British scientific fellowship was Raman elected to in 1924?
options: Knight Commander of the Order of the Bath
questions.explanation: Raman was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society (FRS) in 1924.

questions.q: Raman founded which research institute in Bangalore, which bears his name?
questions.explanation: Raman founded the Raman Research Institute in Bangalore in 1948.

questions.q: Raman became the first Indian director of which institution in 1933?
questions.explanation: In 1933 Raman became the first Indian director of the Indian Institute of Science (IISc), Bangalore.

questions.q: The honorific title 'Sir' was conferred on Raman because he received what British honour in 1929?
questions.explanation: Raman was knighted in 1929, after which he was styled Sir C. V. Raman.

questions.q: The astrophysicist Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar, also a Nobel laureate, was related to Raman as his:
questions.explanation: Nobel laureate Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar was C. V. Raman's nephew.

questions.q: Raman scattering is most commonly studied today using what intense, monochromatic light source?
questions.explanation: Modern Raman spectroscopy uses lasers, which provide the intense monochromatic light needed to detect the weak Raman signal.

questions.q: Raman spectroscopy is widely used today because it reveals information about a sample's:
questions.explanation: Raman spectroscopy probes molecular vibrational modes, making it a powerful tool for identifying chemical structure and composition.

questions.q: In Raman scattering, scattered photons that have lost energy (shifted to lower frequency) produce what type of lines?
questions.explanation: Photons that lose energy to the molecule give Stokes lines (lower frequency); those that gain energy give anti-Stokes lines.

questions.q: The dominant, unshifted scattering of light by molecules (without frequency change) is named after which scientist?
questions.explanation: Elastic, unshifted scattering is Rayleigh scattering; the Raman Effect is the much weaker inelastic component.

questions.q: An analogous inelastic scattering effect for X-rays, discovered earlier, that influenced thinking about the Raman Effect was named after:
questions.explanation: The Compton Effect (inelastic X-ray scattering by electrons) was a conceptual precursor to the optical Raman Effect.

questions.q: In which year was the Raman Effect first theoretically predicted by Adolf Smekal, before Raman observed it experimentally?
questions.explanation: Adolf Smekal predicted inelastic light scattering in 1923, several years before Raman's 1928 experimental confirmation.

questions.q: Besides light scattering, Raman also did notable scientific research on the physics of which musical instruments?
questions.explanation: Early in his career Raman studied the acoustics of Indian musical instruments such as the tabla, mridangam and violin.

questions.q: Raman's explanation of the blue colour of the sea corrected an earlier claim by Lord Rayleigh that the colour was merely:
questions.explanation: Raman showed the sea's blue arises from molecular scattering of light by water, not merely a reflection of the sky as Rayleigh had suggested.

questions.q: Raman reportedly was so confident of winning the Nobel Prize that, well before the 1930 announcement, he booked passage to which event location?
questions.explanation: Raman is famously said to have booked tickets to Stockholm months in advance, confident he would receive the prize awarded there.

questions.q: Roughly what fraction of incident photons undergoes Raman (inelastic) scattering, making the effect notoriously weak?
questions.explanation: Only roughly one in a million scattered photons is Raman-shifted, which is why detecting the effect required careful experiments and now uses lasers.
